content.text: Uniform convergence allows us to exchange limit operations.

content.title: Integration of Uniform Limits
content.content:
If $(f_n)$ converges uniformly to $f$ on $[a, b]$ and each $f_n$ is 
integrable, then $f$ is integrable and
\[
\int_a^b f = \lim_{n \to \infty} \int_a^b f_n.
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Given $\varepsilon > 0$, choose $N$ such that $|f_n(x) - f(x)| < \varepsilon/(b-a)$ 
for $n \geq N$ and all $x$.

Then:
\[
\left|\int_a^b f_n - \int_a^b f\right| \leq \int_a^b |f_n - f| < \frac{\varepsilon}{b-a} \cdot (b-a) = \varepsilon.
\]

content.title: Pointwise Is Not Enough
content.content:
Consider $f_n(x) = n^2 x(1-x)^n$ on $[0, 1]$. Then $f_n \to 0$ pointwise, 
but $\int_0^1 f_n = \frac{n^2}{(n+1)(n+2)} \to 1 \neq 0$.

content.title: Differentiation of Uniform Limits
content.content:
Let $(f_n)$ be a sequence of functions differentiable on $[a, b]$ such that:

1. $(f_n(c))$ converges for some $c \in [a, b]$.

2. $(f_n')$ converges uniformly on $[a, b]$.
Then $(f_n)$ converges uniformly on $[a, b]$ to a differentiable function 
$f$, and $f' = \lim f_n'$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Let $g = \lim f_n'$ (uniform limit). By the FTC and integration theorem:
\[
f_n(x) - f_n(c) = \int_c^x f_n'(t)\,dt \to \int_c^x g(t)\,dt.
\]
Since $f_n(c) \to L$, define $f(x) = L + \int_c^x g$. Then $f$ is 
differentiable and $f' = g$.

content.title: Term-by-Term Integration
content.content:
If $\sum f_n$ converges uniformly on $[a, b]$ to $f$ and each $f_n$ is 
integrable, then
\[
\int_a^b f = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \int_a^b f_n.
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Term-by-Term Differentiation
content.content:
If $(f_n)$ are differentiable on $[a, b]$, $\sum f_n(c)$ converges for 
some $c$, and $\sum f_n'$ converges uniformly, then $\sum f_n$ converges 
uniformly to a differentiable function $f$ with $f' = \sum f_n'$.
content.label: Theorem
